speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 6, September 18, 1996, an interview with Helena Horowitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still under this-- my name. I arrived in Paris. And they-- I know from the joint helped us.

speaker: interviewee
text: They came with taxis. Yes. They picked us up, they-- all the Jewish, whatever, refugees. They brought us to some, you know, place. I slept on the floor, and cry, and, again, cry. And all this was coming. And then I had money. I knew the address. I say they were supposed to-- somebody was supposed to help me. Nobody came. So I walked out. I didn't know how you call a taxi, right? Nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I found my uncle, I found the street without French, without. I come out. And then I waited in front of the house. I see my uncle recognize me. And I waved to him. He didn't know who it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much time did you spend in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: I spend in Paris 5 and 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in that period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle rented a room. He helped, right? He paid for the thing. I immediately started going to the French school at night five times a week. Then I went to a-- I was taking classes in fashion illustration because I was gifted.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- then I started going to a-- a designing school for [INAUDIBLE], you know, pattern making. And I was working. Then I finally-- I didn't want to ask my uncle for money, you know? So I started working as a dress cutter, you know, in the-- in the ready-to-wear. And that's what you did for your time in Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what I was doing in Paris. I had friends. I had something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had relatives here. There was no future in-- I could have gotten married. But it was very difficult in France to establish yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one-- one quick question. What did you learn about the rest of your family, your father, your stepmother and step-- stepbrother and sister, and your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew that my stepmother with the children was sent or they run from Debica ghetto to another ghetto in Bochnia next to something. And I had a letter from them through this Jewish [INAUDIBLE] underground, from the [INAUDIBLE]. And my little sister even wrote. And they wrote me that the little sisters said, ma, how good that Helen is outside so she can survive when we have to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years old. I don't know. My uncle thought-- somebody told me that the children were hidden somewhere in the cloister, and he was looking. But there was no trace.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father supposedly, later in Paris or here, I found out, that he was in Auschwitz. And he was in Plaszow, a few kilometers where I was, unknown to him that I was there. Or maybe he knew. And he died of typhoid fever after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So if we return--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no future there. The French, we are second class citizens, the refugees, the something. I-- my mother's cousin, my mother had two cousins in the United States. One of them was she never knew them, right? They immigrated. One of them was a-- a lawyer in Bridgeport, was one of the first Jews admitted to Yale. So they helped. They sent me this affidavit.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went to a consulate. You know, they wanted me to come as a student. Then he said no. You want to go and get married, probably, and stay. It took me a long time to get my-- my immigration papers. I really did not want to come here, you know, really, you know? This wasn't very inviting, the America, yes. But you did come, and you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did come. I arrived by plane. They even send the plane ticket. And I stayed with my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came here, you went to relatives.

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Brooklyn, Borough Park. I started looking for a job in the garment center. It was very difficult. I really didn't have too much of a good, you know, background in this profession. And I lived with my aunt and her something in Borough Park. I had to-- it was very difficult to commute. Then I got my own apartment in Washington Heights.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were working in the garment--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working on and off. And it was very difficult. And it was a very difficult and, I would say, lonely time. There's dating and this.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband, I met him somewhere. And he lived with his mother. He supported his mother. And he, I got this apart in Washington Heights. There was a Polish Jews organization. They were meeting for some coffees. There's something. Or I knew about of him. But-- and he lived in-- well, he knew me, but we didn't, you know, I was dating. It was a very painful 10 years here, very difficult without any-- any support, you know, emotional, whatever. Or maybe it was just me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one day, he-- I-- every second, I worked for-- did you ever hear of David Crystal? No? A big something like Calvin Klein now, you know? They are out of business. I worked there for four years as assistant designer. And every second Saturday I had to work. We were two assistants. If I didn't work, then I worked Friday night overtime. And I was going from the subway to my apartment. And my husband was going from this. He lived in the neighborhood. And we just crossed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And it was fate. And I was going out with other people. But that was-- somehow, I was choosy. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry your husband? So this was in the-- we were going out for 2 and 1/2 years. And he ask me then to go-- want to go to the movies? So I didn't eat yet. And then we something, you know? I always, you know, that I never called him. But you did marry him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to. Yes, I married him. It was difficult. We were with his mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1962. And I was working. And then my mother's cousin, you should have a child. I was already old, right, for a-- some thing. So, I mean, I-- I-- I was very lucky. Somehow fate was always intervening. I think. I believe in this-- this mazel, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there something else that you wanted to add?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked through my-- so we decided though maybe and she-- I got pregnant, right, just like with no doctors, with no tests, with no something. And when the-- when my daughter was born in 1965, you know, I was working. I couldn't go back to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so and now, I stayed home and doing whatever I could to help. Fred was working hard. And we-- he was his decision to buy a house. I was shocked. It wasn't a house because we were too old to start, right? The house that we bought, this house, his mother lived with us. And I was working then. I stopped working when my daughter was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you've had a very productive life because even in the background--

speaker: interviewer
text: --we can see some of your artwork.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was still denigrating or whatever, putting my myself down, that I didn't work outside, that I stopped working.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a question. It's been well over 50 years since the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have told a very full--

speaker: interviewer
text: --of what you experienced and of what you are still experiencing. Is there any message or any statement that you would like to make for future generations who might see this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I said that it's wrong. I mean there's discrimination with the race. It's so unfounded. And I have to say that when I went to this DP camp, with non-Jewish DP camp, that was a biggest shock of my life because I realized-- I don't know if I should say this. Is, you know, politics or something. They were-- they were-- I was, shall I say, superior. I was better in classes than the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was no ubermenschen there. Who were they to step over us, you know? There were so many of those Ukrainians and things and something. I didn't see anything that was better than the Jews, right? They had their whatever. And who had the right to-- to-- you know, to put us down. That they shouldn't be any race. And then another thing, why we didn't resist? There was no crime. Nobody had knives. Nobody knew how to-- to stop anybody. And this condition didn't come with Hitler. It was going on for years that we were being put down by the Poles or Russians or pogroms or something. So we were already this-- always-- always down. So-- Well--

speaker: interviewee
text: --unfortunately, you know, I-- psychologically, I was damaged. I had depressions. I had to get, you know, whatever. Always comes back.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So I thought this life here was an ordinary-- I mean ordinary. I was working, and doing everything home, and painting walls, and gardening, and cooking, and parties. I became a big hostess, no more. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh behalf of the Shoah Foundation--

speaker: interviewer
text: --I want to thank you for this interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I apologize for this long talk. Think your papers are falling off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Helena, would you introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This is my husband, my very wonderful husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fred Horowitz, Fred Horowitz. He-- he nursed me when I had operations. He helps me in my art. He was doing things for me. And we had a good life. We traveled a lot together. And he was a wonderful father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we are married already since 1962. It's a long time. We had, I feel, very good marriage. She was a wonderful wife. And you were here when I was sick. She took good care of me. And until now, she's really taking good care of me as far special diet and special type of life we have right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have one daughter. She lives in Philadelphia. Would be nice if she would be with us-- with us. That's the way I feel. However, she has a good job there. She likes her job. She's probably better off living in Philadelphia. It's more livable city than New York. That's her choice. But she comes to visit us. We talk every day on the phone. And we are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, both of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. I-- I was given this picture here from the relatives. I think she's 1916 She was 18 years old. | is me, the fur coat, and my brother, Isaac in Debica. I-- maybe I was six or five. This was taken under the occupation in the-- before the ghetto, but in the war. This is my-- this is me and my little stepsister and stepbrother. They all perished. They perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who took this picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: And gave it to a relative?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Or maybe I had this picture with me because it didn't look like, you know. This-- this man's name-- this is Lambert Grutszh, a Tyrolvian, Austrian, who really saved my life. He knew of my true identity and he maybe faced prison if he was revealed. But regardless, he went with me to the Labor Department and he asked for me as his mate. I got papers. And he-- I went with him to Austria, to Tyrol, during the war, end of the war when all the concentration camps. And I survived working as a farm hand with his family. I was never treated as a maid. They didn't know. He didn't tell his family about my identity. This is my arbeitskarte. This is my ID as a foreign worker. This is not a-- a false paper. This was given to me by the German Labor Department. And this is like a passport. This was a passport and the permission to go to Austria and to work as a-- on the farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the original which you saved from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the original that I saved from the war that I obtained when-- before Lambert took me out. This is-- I-- this is me working on this farm in the Alps as a farm hand. But I was like a member of the family. I worked like a man. There, you see me with a basket on my back. I carried all different kinds, including soil in the fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was milking cows, working on the something. And there, all this, you know, I survived there, hidden, sheltered from camps and things. This is my uncle, my father's brother, who survived as a soldier, as a prisoner of war in Hungary. And he was unaware what happened to his family. He came back to Poland after the war to his town. And he found-- he heard hearsay that I escaped. And he started looking for me. And he traced me to the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And through him-- he died two years ago at 94.

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a very hard life. He lost a wife and a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he saved you at the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he helped me. Yeah. This is me in Paris. I am dressed. I worked in the garment industry. And fashion was very important in Paris. I spent 5 and 1/2 years in Paris. This is my husband, I, and my daughter Tina on vacation. We traveled a lot with her.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- yeah. Fred, he's a mechanical engineer. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --recently retired after receiving a medal from the Port Authority after 42 years of very productive and creative work, so-- that's me. I painted when I stayed home. I painted silk scarves and I had an exhibit for two months in the local library.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I also made pillows and water color, oil. This is one of my silk-painted scarves. I made many scarves. This was painted on white. And I have many other scarves. But that was that I liked best. One, two, three.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is my daughter, Tina, one and only child, very bright. She studied the University of Pennsylvania, And this is-- she received a law degree from Temple Law School in Philadelphia. And she works in Philadelphia in Wharton as a research coordinator. That's enough? Thank you, again.
